costs: Fiscal cost of statutory transport guarantees and TEIP-style compensatory programming
Administrative cost of alternatives-assessment documentation for every proposed closure
Potential legislative negotiation cost given documented political sensitivity of closures
content.equity_impact: Mixed but structurally improved over status quo: formal consultation and transport guarantees reduce arbitrary harm distribution, but consolidation itself may still concentrate disadvantage and reduce accessible options most for poorer, lower-mobility and Roma families unless the compensatory and non-discrimination provisions are rigorously enforced (F-live-education_finance-06, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07).
expected_benefits: Removes current legal ambiguity over transport responsibility, reducing risk of uncompensated harm to lower-income families (F-live-legal_and_governance-05)
Provides procedural consistency and transparency currently absent from ad hoc administrative closure decisions (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)
Creates auditable non-discrimination check against catchment-based segregation consistent with ECtHR jurisprudence (F-live-portuguese_reform-09)
Acknowledges and manages unavoidable zero-enrollment cases with dignity rather than contested indefinite retention
content.goal: Where village schools are demonstrably no longer viable (including zero-enrollment cliff cases), close them through a formal, transparent procedure with binding transport guarantees, targeted disadvantage compensation, and consultation rights, rather than through the current ad hoc administrative closure practice.

implementation_steps.action: Enact a statutory closure procedure specifying required alternatives-assessment documentation, consultation timelines, and mandatory transport guarantees
Issue implementing regulations clarifying transport-obligation assignment and minimum service frequency standards for consolidated destination schools
Apply the new procedure to pending and future closure decisions, including formal documentation of alternatives considered
Design and fund TEIP-style targeted support for pupils displaced to consolidated schools, monitored for segregation effects
Track segregation and disadvantage-concentration indicators at consolidated destination schools post-implementation

mechanisms: Scotland's presumption-against-closure model requires formal documented assessment of alternatives, educational benefits, community effects and travel arrangements before any closure — a template for procedural rigor even where Hungary does not adopt an outright presumption (F-live-international_comparison-02).

The legal ambiguity over transport-provision responsibility (municipality vs. school district) documented by the ombudsman must be resolved by statute to prevent transport gaps that disproportionately burden lower-income households (F-live-legal_and_governance-05, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10).

Free public transport for children under 14 introduced in 2023 provides a partial foundation but rural frequency gaps remain unaddressed and must be closed via route guarantees (F-live-implementation_planning-09, F-live-school_network_planning-07).

Given ECtHR (Szolcsán v. Hungary 2023) and ongoing EU infringement proceedings on segregation, any consolidated destination school must be subject to non-discrimination catchment review to prevent reproducing segregation via closure (F-live-portuguese_reform-09, F-live-implementation_planning-07).

Zero-enrollment cliff cases (e.g., Garab) demonstrate that in some settlements closure is not a policy choice but a demographic fact, requiring dignified/managed closure procedures analogous to Austria's 'gestures of reconciliation' model rather than contested viability debate (F-live-demography-05, F-live-international_comparison-08).
risks: Consolidation can accelerate local depopulation according to Finnish and Danish quasi-experimental evidence, an effect not yet tested for Hungary (F-live-international_comparison-01, F-live-demography-07, F-live-portuguese_reform-05)

Closure decisions have historically provoked political costs and reversals (Swedish evidence on trust decline; Hungarian precedent for protest-driven reversal), risking policy instability if mitigation is perceived as insufficient (F-live-international_comparison-06, F-live-political_feasibility-07)
Consolidated destination schools may face 'association pressure' cascades affecting neighboring villages not originally targeted (F-live-school_network_planning-05)
content.title: Consolidation with statutory mitigation: closure as managed default plus transport, equity and consultation guarantees
